source.title: Reinforcement Learning, Fast and Slow
source.venue: Trends in Cognitive Sciences, vol 23(5), pp 408-422

confidence_rationale: This review paper synthesises two complementary mechanisms for fast learning that directly map to REE's architecture: episodic memory (cross-episode regularity extraction = MECH-316) and meta-RL (within-episode adaptation = Wang 2018 entry). It explicitly frames both as solutions to the slow-RL sample-complexity problem, which is what REE's bottom-up rule discovery is also trying to solve. Confidence not higher because the paper is a review rather than original empirical work, and because the clean fast/slow dichotomy may oversimplify the more complex multi-timescale picture Pull 2 R3 surfaced (online + offline + sleep-mediated consolidation).
failure_signatures: If REE's MECH-316 cross-episode-regularity-extraction does not produce the same kind of episodic-memory-mediated fast-learning signature (rapid generalisation to novel tasks based on stored episode memories) the Botvinick 2019 episodic-RL framing predicts, the inheritance pathway weakens.

The fast/slow dichotomy may collapse REE's three-timescale structure (online + within-episode + sleep-mediated consolidation) into two; if so, REE has a finer-grained timing structure than the literature, which would be a candidate KEEP-AS-IS for R3.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Deep RL is sample-inefficient because gradient-based weight updates are slow. Two complementary mechanisms allow fast learning despite slow gradients: (i) episodic memory, where stored episodes are retrieved and used to bootstrap rapid generalisation, and (ii) meta-learning, where slow training over a task distribution shapes a network into one capable of fast within-episode adaptation. Both mechanisms have neural counterparts: hippocampal episodic memory and prefrontal meta-RL respectively. Together they account for human-like sample-efficient learning.
mapping.ree_translation: The Botvinick 2019 fast/slow framing maps cleanly to REE's planned cluster: MECH-316 (cross-episode-regularity-extraction, hippocampal-monosynaptic-CLS-analog) is the *episodic-memory fast-learning* mechanism; ARC-062 + MECH-318 (per Wang 2018 / RL^2 collapse) is the *meta-RL fast-learning* mechanism. The two mechanisms are complementary, not competing -- exactly what Pull 2 R3 (online + offline both needed) said. The vocabulary inheritance: 'episodic RL' for MECH-316, 'meta RL' for ARC-062 + MECH-318.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The fast/slow dichotomy is a useful organising structure but may oversimplify REE's three-timescale picture. Pull 2 R3 identified online (within-episode), online-quiet-period (awake replay), and offline (sleep consolidation) as three distinct timescales. Botvinick 2019 collapses the latter two. Whether the third timescale is genuinely architecturally distinct (KEEP-AS-IS) or a refinement of the existing two (RENAME) is left open by this paper.
mapping.source_context: Top-tier review venue (Trends Cog Sci). Synthesises ML and neuroscience literature; no original empirical work.

confidence_components.notes: Top-tier review, well-cited. Mapping fidelity is high for the cluster-level fast/slow split. Transfer risk low-moderate because review papers transfer well as vocabulary-source documents.
